topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stood in the sterile, brightly lit office, the scent of old paper and lemon polish suddenly feeling suffocating. The framed photograph on the desk, the one of her mentor, Dr. Alistair Vance, smiling warmly in his lab coat, seemed to mock the silence. She had been expecting a call, an email, anything to signal his departure, but the phone remained stubbornly silent. A knot tightened in her stomach, a cold, heavy sensation spreading through her chest. She picked up the desk phone, her fingers hovering over the receiver before she finally lowered it, the plastic cool beneath her palm. "Hello?" Her voice sounded thin, reedy. Only the monotonous ring of the dial tone answered. She slowly pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. She needed an explanation, a simple note, anything to make sense of this abrupt void.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the empty chair across the mahogany conference table. The silence in the usually bustling mentorship room was deafening. He kept glancing at the door, half-expecting Alistair to materialize, perhaps with a hastily scribbled farewell note tucked into his jacket pocket. Mark ran a hand through his already disheveled hair, the gesture jerky and uncontrolled. He replayed the last few weeks in his mind—the late-night consultations, the shared breakthroughs, the easy camaraderie—and found nothing concrete. He stood up, pacing the length of the room twice, his boots clicking sharply on the polished wood. When he finally stopped by the window, looking out at the indifferent city skyline, a deep, hollow ache settled in his throat. It felt like a piece of his own foundation had just been quietly removed.

[story 3]

"He's not answering." Sarah whispered to herself, the sound barely audible above the hum of the ventilation system. She circled the mentor's office, her movements slow and deliberate, as if navigating a minefield. Every object seemed too neat, too undisturbed. On the shelf, the award for outstanding achievement remained, gleaming under the fluorescent lights, utterly untouched. Sarah leaned against the doorframe, her arms crossed tightly over her chest, a slight tremor running through her shoulders. She tried calling his personal line again, pressing the buttons repeatedly, listening only to the dead, flat tone that confirmed her growing unease. A strange, unsettling stillness had settled over the entire space, a quiet that screamed louder than any shouted goodbye.
